name: Satellite-based Land-Cover Classification & Mapping
description: This domain covers deep learning approaches for classifying land cover and land use from multispectral, hyperspectral, and high-resolution satellite and UAV imagery, spanning objects and ecosystems such as aquaculture ponds, mangroves, forests, farmlands, and urban greenspace. It emphasizes methodological challenges including data augmentation, dataset creation for semantic segmentation, self-supervised and multiple-instance learning, and assessing data limitations. Case studies range from region-specific datasets like IndiaSat to greenspace quantification in Karachi and LULC change mapping in Rwanda.

background_concepts.why_needed: These readings classify land cover from satellite imagery whose input channels are spectral bands (red, NIR, etc.); the papers assume readers understand how surfaces reflect different wavelengths but never explain it.

suggested_resources.title: NASA Earthdata: Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI)
GIS Geography: What is NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index)?

background_concepts.why_needed: The clustering-augmented land cover paper builds on self-supervised representation learning to reduce label needs, but assumes the reader already knows the pretext-task paradigm.

suggested_resources.title: Self-supervised learning for vision foundation models (Zeeshan Khan Suri)

name: Remote Sensing for Deforestation & Forest Change Detection
description: This domain focuses on detecting and measuring forest and land change over time using multi-temporal satellite and radar imagery, including reviews of Earth observation methods in the tropics and applications like selective logging and reforestation detection. It features deep learning and time-series techniques such as BFAST, echo state networks, cascade frameworks, and the Segment Anything Model for change detection. Applications extend from the Brazilian Amazon and Kenya to fine-resolution vegetation monitoring and detection of destroyed settlements in Nigeria.

background_concepts.concept: BFAST (Breaks For Additive Season and Trend) time-series decomposition
background_concepts.why_needed: The multi-type forest change detection paper builds directly on BFAST applied to Landsat time series but does not explain how the algorithm decomposes a signal into trend/season/remainder to flag breakpoints.

suggested_resources.title: BFAST project overview (r-forge)
SEPAL documentation: Time series analysis with BFAST

background_concepts.why_needed: The selective-logging and human-driven alteration papers fuse radar with optical imagery because SAR penetrates cloud/canopy; the readings assume familiarity with active radar sensing and backscatter.

suggested_resources.title: NASA Earthdata: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)
NASA ARSET: An Introduction to SAR and Its Applications (Part 1)

name: Forest Carbon & Biomass Estimation via Remote Sensing
description: This domain covers quantifying forest carbon stocks, fluxes, aboveground biomass, and canopy height using remote sensing tools such as GEDI laser ranging, Landsat, and SAR combined with machine learning. Topics include global carbon flux mapping, uncertainty-aware estimation via mixture density networks, geospatial foundation models, and carbon sequestration assessment including urban forests and tree age discrimination. It also incorporates sensing hardware like the Fluxbot CO2 flux chamber and broader reviews of AI in sustainable forestry.

background_concepts.concept: LiDAR / laser altimetry for canopy height
background_concepts.why_needed: The GEDI/canopy-height and biomass papers depend on spaceborne LiDAR to measure vertical forest structure but assume the reader knows how laser ranging retrieves height and structure.

suggested_resources.title: NSF NEON: The Basics of LiDAR - Light Detection and Ranging
NASA ARSET: The Fundamentals of LiDAR

background_concepts.concept: Aboveground biomass and carbon flux/stock accounting
background_concepts.why_needed: These readings estimate aboveground biomass and convert it to carbon stocks and flux, assuming the reader understands the biomass-to-carbon conversion and sink/source terminology.

suggested_resources.title: NASA Earthdata / SAR Handbook: SAR Methods for Mapping and Monitoring Forest Biomass

name: Carbon Offset & REDD+ Integrity
description: This domain examines the credibility and accuracy of forest carbon offset and REDD+ programs, highlighting problems such as systematic over-crediting in California's program and overstated emission reductions from voluntary REDD+ projects in the Brazilian Amazon. It also addresses machine learning benchmarks for equitable monitoring, reporting, and verification of nature-based solutions.

background_concepts.why_needed: Both over-crediting papers argue that credits exceed real reductions relative to a baseline; understanding additionality and baseline scenarios is essential to follow their critique.

suggested_resources.title: Carbon Offset Guide: Additionality
Carbon Market Watch: Additionality & Baselines

background_concepts.concept: REDD+ mechanism (MRV, forest reference emission levels)
background_concepts.why_needed: The voluntary REDD+ paper assumes readers know the REDD+ crediting framework, its results-based payment logic, and reference emission levels.

suggested_resources.title: UNFCCC: What is REDD+?

name: Community-Based Forest Governance, Tenure & Participatory Mapping
description: This domain addresses community management of forests through the lens of land tenure, payment for environmental services, and participatory approaches to mapping and governance. It includes deep learning applications for transparent PES communication, machine learning for coviability transition pathways, and participatory mapping combined with satellite imagery, as illustrated by cases in India and the Chunati Wildlife Sanctuary.

name: Community Climate Vulnerability & Adaptation Assessment Tools
description: This domain covers tools and methods for assessing community vulnerability and resilience to climate change, emphasizing participatory and community-driven evaluation processes such as the CoDriVE-Programme Designer and Commons Connect application. It includes natural resource management consultation, retrospective resilience assessment of semi-arid farming systems in India, geospatial and AI-based flood and deforestation vulnerability mapping, and co-designing microclimate sensors with communities.

name: Public Works & Social Protection as Carbon Co-Benefits
description: This domain explores the intersection of India's MGNREGA social protection and public works programs with climate mitigation, focusing on the carbon sequestration co-benefits achieved and potentially achievable through such schemes. It also examines pro-poor climate policy innovations, small-scale infrastructure, and land-use changes around mining sites in India.

background_concepts.why_needed: These carbon co-benefit studies quantify sequestration from MGNREGA works (water conservation, afforestation) and assume the reader knows the scheme's structure and permissible works.

suggested_resources.title: Vikaspedia: Overview of MGNREGA

name: Nature-Based Solutions & Ecosystem Valuation
description: This domain covers the valuation and planning of nature-based solutions and ecosystems, including causal frameworks for large-scale ecosystem valuation such as wetland benefits for flood mitigation. It incorporates landscape ecology metrics in sustainability planning, machine learning methods like adaptive Dice loss for wetland delineation and deep Gaussian processes for mitigation decision support, alongside critical assessment of the value and limits of nature-based solutions.

background_concepts.why_needed: The wetlands causal-valuation and NbS papers assume readers understand the concept of quantifying ecosystem services (provisioning, regulating) as monetary or welfare benefits.

suggested_resources.title: USGS: LandCarbon / ecosystem carbon sequestration assessment overview

name: Biodiversity & Conservation Planning Optimization
description: This domain focuses on optimization approaches for conservation and biodiversity planning, including derivative-free optimization frameworks for land conservation decisions and optimizing trees for carbon sequestration. It also addresses constraint-based spatial planning to improve graph connectivity in pursuit of the 30-by-30 Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework target.

background_concepts.why_needed: The connectivity optimization paper frames its objective around the '30 by 30' target of the global biodiversity framework, an institutional mechanism it references without introducing.

suggested_resources.title: UNFCCC / IPCC land-use and biodiversity context (REDD+ land-use page)

name: Deforestation Detection & Driver Classification from Satellite Imagery
description: This domain covers detecting deforestation and classifying its underlying drivers from satellite imagery using deep learning, including vision transformers (ForestViT), rotation-equivariant segmentation, and interpretable and lightweight attribution models. Applications include forecasting deforestation in the Amazon, tracking illegal cattle ranching, classifying deforestation drivers in Indonesia via ForestNet, and analyzing climate risks to soy-maize cropping from Amazon deforestation.

background_concepts.concept: Vision Transformers vs. CNNs for image classification
background_concepts.why_needed: Driver-classification papers (e.g. ForestViT, rotation-equivariant models) assume the reader knows the architectural distinction between convolutional networks and transformer-based image classifiers, and why equivariance/attention matter for overhead imagery.

suggested_resources.title: Beginner's Guide to Semantic Segmentation (covers CNN backbones)

name: Forest Carbon Stock Estimation & Carbon Credit Integrity
description: This domain focuses on quantifying forest carbon and ensuring the reliability of carbon credits, using approaches like Bayesian state-space models for deforestation baselines and deep learning with aerial imagery to address overestimation of forest carbon. It also covers AI-driven methods for automating carbon sequestration site assessment and joint industry-university efforts to tackle carbon storage challenges. Collectively, the readings aim to improve the accuracy and trustworthiness of forest carbon accounting and credit markets.

background_concepts.why_needed: Deforestation-baseline and carbon-credit papers rely on estimating a counterfactual (what would have happened without protection), often via synthetic control or state-space causal methods, which are used but not taught.

suggested_resources.title: Introduction to Synthetic Control Methods (Tilburg Science Hub)
Synthetic control — Causal Inference Course notes

name: Individual Tree & Canopy Structure Measurement
description: This domain covers techniques for measuring tree-level and canopy attributes, including species classification from LiDAR, satellite, and 3D tomographic SAR data, DBH estimation from depth maps, and canopy height and cover mapping at scales from Chicago to India. It also encompasses segmenting dead trees, reconstructing tree structure, assessing urban forest health from street view imagery, and geographically localizing natural objects like palms. Together the readings emphasize fine-grained detection, classification, and structural measurement of trees using diverse remote and ground-level sensing.

background_concepts.why_needed: The tomographic-SAR tree-species paper relies on 3D SAR to resolve vertical canopy structure; readers need baseline SAR sensing knowledge the paper does not provide.

suggested_resources.title: NASA Earthdata: Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)

name: Foundation Models & Data-Efficient Learning for Earth Observation
description: This domain explores large-scale pretrained and multi-modal foundation models for remote sensing, including forest monitoring models (FoMo), climatic and geographic location encoders, and masked autoencoders for data reconstruction. It also addresses data-efficient strategies such as visual prompt-tuning, few-shot instance segmentation, weakly-semi-supervised detection, data programming for labeling, and fine-tuning language models for biodiversity entity recognition. The readings collectively aim to reduce labeled-data requirements and build general-purpose models for ecological and Earth observation tasks.

background_concepts.concept: Foundation models, self-supervision, and masked autoencoders
background_concepts.why_needed: These papers build geospatial foundation models and masked-autoencoder pretraining, assuming the reader knows the pretraining/fine-tuning paradigm and pretext tasks like masked reconstruction.

suggested_resources.title: Introduction to Earth Foundation Models (IGARSS 2025 tutorial)
Self-supervised learning for vision foundation models (Zeeshan Khan Suri)

name: Vegetation & Ecosystem Dynamics Forecasting
description: This domain concerns modeling and forecasting vegetation and ecosystem behavior over time, including forest greenness monitoring, phenology prediction, net primary productivity under warming, and drought resilience analysis via Shapley values. It spans data-driven and mechanistic approaches, hybrid and physics-encoded spatiotemporal deep learning, and datasets/challenges like EarthNet2021 for forecasting localized climate impacts. The readings emphasize predicting how vegetation and land surfaces respond to climatic drivers across regions such as Africa and India.

background_concepts.why_needed: Forecasting greenness and phenology relies on time series of NDVI and related indices as the target variable; the papers assume knowledge of what greenness metrics measure.

suggested_resources.title: NASA Earthdata: Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI)

name: Community Co-Design & Indigenous Knowledge in Climate Monitoring
description: This domain centers on incorporating community perspectives and Indigenous knowledge into climate monitoring and resilience efforts. The reading focuses on capturing and digitizing Indigenous knowledge to strengthen community resilience to climate change, highlighting participatory and co-design approaches to climate data.

name: Interpretability & Verification for Climate Finance and Conservation
description: This domain addresses making machine learning for climate finance and forest conservation transparent, trustworthy, and verifiable, as in GainForest's interpretable models on satellite imagery and Xingu's explanations of geospatial predictions under weak supervision. It also covers verification methods like TrueBranch's metric-learning-based checks on conservation projects and systems designed to be past- and future-aware when evaluating climate policies. Together the readings emphasize interpretability, accountability, and validation to support credible climate finance decisions.

background_concepts.concept: Metric learning and weak supervision for verification
background_concepts.why_needed: Verification systems (TrueBranch, Xingu, GainForest) rely on metric-learning embeddings and weakly-supervised labels to audit conservation claims, assuming the reader knows these representation-learning and label-efficient techniques.

suggested_resources.title: A Primer on Self-supervised Learning (covers representation/metric learning)
